content.text: An AI operating model defines how authority, resources, and accountability for AI initiatives are distributed across an enterprise. The hub-and-spoke model places a central AI Center of Excellence (CoE) at the hub and assigns embedded AI practitioners to business units (the spokes), with all significant decisions flowing through the center. The federated model grants business domains autonomous AI teams that own their own roadmaps, tooling choices, and delivery cycles, while a lightweight standards council enforces interoperability and risk guardrails across the federation. Most large enterprises fall on a spectrum between these two poles, and the practical choice is where to set the dial given the firm's size, culture, and stage of AI maturity.

Choosing the wrong operating model is one of the most expensive structural mistakes an enterprise AI program can make. A hub-and-spoke model imposed on a large, diverse conglomerate creates a CoE that becomes a bottleneck within 18 months: every domain waits in the same queue, priorities conflict, and the center loses credibility. A federated model imposed too early — before shared infrastructure, model risk management, and data governance exist — produces duplicated tooling, inconsistent risk posture, and shadow AI proliferating in untraceable notebooks. The operating model decision shapes every downstream choice: hiring, budgeting, vendor contracts, and audit scope.
Hub-and-spoke delivers consistent governance: every model deployed meets the same risk, security, and quality bar because a single team owns the standards.
Hub-and-spoke accelerates early programs: shared infrastructure (model catalog, vector stores, observability) is built once and reused by all spokes.
Federated model enables domain velocity: business units iterate on use cases without waiting for central approval, reducing time-to-production for mature teams.
Federated model produces domain-fit solutions: finance, legal, and operations have different data environments and regulatory contexts; autonomous teams can optimize for their domain.
Hybrid models combine both: a shared platform layer provides foundational capabilities while domain teams own use case delivery, capturing economies of scale without sacrificing speed.
Hub-and-spoke becomes a bottleneck at scale: a CoE of 20 engineers cannot serve 50 business units simultaneously; throughput caps out and business units route around the center.
Hub-and-spoke limits domain ownership: spoke teams feel like consumers rather than builders, reducing motivation to develop deep AI capability at the domain level.
Federated model risks fragmentation: without disciplined standards enforcement, each domain builds its own data pipelines, model registries, and evaluation frameworks.
Federated model requires mature domain teams: it assumes each domain has ML engineers, data scientists, and AI product managers — talent most domains lack in early programs.
Both models require explicit governance charters: absence of a documented model means each operating decision becomes a negotiation, consuming leadership time and delaying delivery.

In the hub-and-spoke model, the central CoE owns model selection, infrastructure provisioning, security architecture, model risk management, and tooling standards. Embedded AI practitioners ('spoke engineers') live in business units, translating domain requirements into project briefs that the CoE reviews and prioritizes. The CoE assigns engineers to approved projects, builds the capability, and hands it off to the spoke for operations. This creates a clean separation between capability building (CoE) and business context (spoke), but it concentrates all decision-making authority in the center.

In the federated model, each domain has a self-sufficient AI team with product, engineering, and data science. A thin Standards Council — typically four to eight members drawn from domain leads and the CISO — publishes binding standards for model risk classification, data residency, API contracts between domain systems, and minimum evaluation benchmarks. Domain teams sign off on compliance before production deployment. The council does not review individual projects; it audits compliance quarterly.

The practical hybrid pattern that most large enterprises adopt is a 'platform hub, delivery federation' structure: a central Platform Engineering team owns the shared AI platform (LLM gateway, vector infrastructure, observability, MLOps toolchain), while domain teams own use case delivery end-to-end within that platform. This gives domains autonomy over what they build without requiring them to rebuild foundational infrastructure independently.

attrs.alt: Hub-and-Spoke vs Federated AI Operating Model comparison diagram

content.text: The left panel shows hub-and-spoke: a central CoE connects to five business unit spokes (Finance, HR, Ops, Legal, Sales). The right panel shows the federated model: a thin Standards Council sits above three autonomous domain AI teams that all connect to a shared infrastructure layer below them.

Choose hub-and-spoke when your AI program is in its first 18 months, you have fewer than five active AI use cases, your risk and compliance infrastructure is immature, or your business units lack ML engineering talent. The center provides the scaffolding that business units cannot yet build for themselves. Choose federated when you have 10+ active AI teams, domain AI budgets are controlled at the business unit level, and you have at least two cycles of experience enforcing enterprise standards. The hybrid 'platform hub, delivery federation' model is appropriate when you have a mature platform team and at least three domain teams with established engineering practices.
Audit current state: catalog every AI initiative by business unit, team size, model type, data sensitivity, and regulatory scope. This surfaces the de facto operating model and identifies shadow AI.

Define the target model: use a decision matrix covering AI maturity score, number of active teams, compliance complexity, and talent distribution. Assign a target point on the centralization spectrum.

Charter the CoE or Standards Council: document its scope, authority, membership, meeting cadence, and decision rights. Distinguish what the council mandates (non-negotiable standards) from what it recommends (preferred patterns).

Migrate progressively: for hub-to-federated transitions, transfer ownership of mature use cases to domain teams before cutting central resources. Never reduce the hub capacity before domain teams demonstrate readiness.

Measure and adapt: track delivery lead time, number of production incidents per team, standards compliance scores, and CoE backlog depth. Review model fit annually and adjust when metrics diverge from targets.

# Hub-and-spoke: CoE project intake scoring
# Business unit submits a use case; CoE scores it for prioritization

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class UseCaseProposal:
    title: str
    business_unit: str
    estimated_annual_value_usd: float
    data_sensitivity: str          # 'public' | 'internal' | 'confidential' | 'restricted'
    regulatory_scope: list
    required_model_type: str       # 'generative' | 'predictive' | 'agentic'
    requesting_team_ml_headcount: int

def score_for_coe_prioritization(proposal):
    score = 0
    if proposal.estimated_annual_value_usd > 5_000_000:
        score += 30
    elif proposal.estimated_annual_value_usd > 1_000_000:
        score += 15
    regulatory_weight = len(proposal.regulatory_scope) * 10
    score += min(regulatory_weight, 30)
    if proposal.requesting_team_ml_headcount >= 3:
        ownership = 'domain_team'
        score -= 10
    else:
        ownership = 'coe_led'
        score += 10
    tier = 'P1' if score >= 50 else 'P2' if score >= 25 else 'P3'
    return {'total_score': score, 'recommended_ownership': ownership, 'priority_tier': tier}

proposal = UseCaseProposal(
    title='Automated contract review agent',
    business_unit='Legal',
    estimated_annual_value_usd=2_500_000,
    data_sensitivity='confidential',
    regulatory_scope=['GDPR', 'SOX'],
    required_model_type='agentic',
    requesting_team_ml_headcount=0
)
print(score_for_coe_prioritization(proposal))
# Output: {'total_score': 55, 'recommended_ownership': 'coe_led', 'priority_tier': 'P1'}

# Federated model: standards compliance check before production deployment
# Each domain team runs this gate in their CI pipeline

import json, sys
from pathlib import Path

def check_standards_compliance(model_card_path):
    violations = []
    card = json.loads(Path(model_card_path).read_text())
    required_fields = [
        'model_risk_tier',       # 'low' | 'medium' | 'high'
        'data_residency',
        'evaluation_benchmark',
        'evaluation_score',
        'human_review_threshold',
        'incident_runbook_url'
    ]
    for field in required_fields:
        if field not in card:
            violations.append(f'Missing required field: {field}')
    tier = card.get('model_risk_tier', '')
    score = card.get('evaluation_score', 0)
    thresholds = {'low': 0.70, 'medium': 0.82, 'high': 0.90}
    if tier in thresholds and score < thresholds[tier]:
        violations.append(
            f'Score {score:.2f} below {thresholds[tier]:.2f} for {tier}-risk tier'
        )
    return violations

violations = check_standards_compliance('model_card.json')
if violations:
    print('BLOCKED: Standards violations:')
    for v in violations:
        print(f'  - {v}')
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print('PASSED: All standards checks passed')

A global financial services firm with 45,000 employees launched its generative AI program using a strict hub-and-spoke model: a central AI CoE of 30 engineers owned all model selection, infrastructure, and deployment decisions. Within 12 months, the CoE had a backlog of 60 approved use cases with an average wait time of 8 months from approval to production. High-performing business units began deploying models outside the CoE's visibility, a pattern the CISO discovered during a vendor audit.

The firm's response was a controlled transition to a hybrid model over 9 months. The CoE was reorganized into a Platform Engineering team that owned the LLM gateway, model registry, and observability stack. Each of the four main business divisions received a charter to build a domain AI team of 4-8 engineers, with the Platform team providing onboarding, tooling, and quarterly compliance reviews. By month 18 of the transition, production deployment lead time fell from 8 months to 6 weeks, and the shadow AI inventory was zero because domain teams had a legitimate path to production.

Federating before infrastructure is shared: domain teams each build their own vector stores, model registries, and evaluation pipelines, creating a support nightmare when any component needs upgrading.
Treating the CoE as a permanent service desk: the hub should be a capability builder that transfers ownership, not a permanent delivery team for every project.

Not chartering the Standards Council's authority: federated models collapse when domains can simply ignore council standards — enforcement mechanisms (CI gates, deployment approvals) must be specified in the charter.

Copying another firm's model without assessing fit: a model that works at a cloud-native tech company will not work at a regulated bank; structural differences in risk appetite, talent, and audit requirements all matter.
Failing to review the model annually: operating model fit changes as AI maturity grows; a hub-and-spoke that was correct at month 12 may be throttling delivery by month 36.
Start centralized, plan federated: build shared infrastructure in the hub phase so federation can inherit it rather than rebuild it.
Define a domain readiness checklist: domain teams earn autonomy by demonstrating they have ML engineers, MLOps tooling, and incident response plans before the hub steps back.
Separate platform from delivery: the platform hub provides infrastructure services; delivery ownership moves to domains. Never merge these into a single team.
Make standards machine-enforceable: embed compliance checks in CI pipelines rather than relying on self-reporting — federated governance only works when enforcement is automated.
Track the shadow AI proxy: if the number of undeclared AI tools in use is increasing, it means the operating model is creating too much friction — adjust the model, not the users.

Operating model performance is measured through delivery metrics, not technical metrics. Key indicators include deployment lead time (from project approval to production), CoE backlog depth, number of active production AI systems per full-time AI engineer, and standards compliance rates across the portfolio. For hub-and-spoke, a healthy CoE should sustain a project throughput of at least one new production deployment per engineer per quarter. When this rate falls below 0.5, the hub is undersized relative to demand or is doing too much delivery work rather than enabling work.

For federated models, scalability comes from the platform layer's ability to onboard new domain teams without proportional increases in platform team headcount. A well-designed shared platform should support 2-3x growth in domain teams with less than 30% growth in platform headcount, achieved through self-service tooling, automated onboarding, and comprehensive internal documentation.

Hub-and-spoke concentrates security responsibility in the CoE, making it straightforward to enforce uniform access controls, data handling policies, and model deployment security. The risk is a single point of failure: a compromise or outage of the CoE disrupts all AI delivery. Federated models distribute the security surface — each domain team must maintain their own secure model deployment pipelines, secrets management, and access controls. The Standards Council must mandate security baselines (no model secrets in git, required network isolation for high-risk models, mandatory prompt injection testing) and verify them through automated scanning.

Reliability governance differs by model: hub-and-spoke centralizes incident response (the CoE owns the runbook for all production AI systems), while federated models require each domain to maintain its own incident response capability. For federated programs, the Standards Council should mandate a minimum incident response capability: on-call rotation, defined escalation path, and a tested runbook for each production system, verified annually.
Hub-and-spoke works for early programs but becomes a bottleneck above 10 active use cases; plan your transition to hybrid or federated from the start.
Federated models require shared infrastructure — federation of delivery without federation of infrastructure creates fragmentation and doubles tooling costs.
The hybrid 'platform hub, delivery federation' model is the most common choice for mature enterprises: it balances governance consistency with delivery speed.
Standards enforcement must be automated — federated governance without machine-checkable standards gates reverts to informal consensus, which is not governance.
Shadow AI is a symptom of operating model friction: if teams route around the model to ship, the model needs to change, not the teams.
Review operating model fit annually using delivery lead time and CoE backlog depth as primary signals — these metrics surface structural problems before they become organizational crises.
